explicit_text: I was born in Akita Prefecture, a place known for its beautiful women called Akita Bijin. I didn't stay long in Japan when I visited with my husband because my relatives there were troublesome. Akita is in the northeastern part of Japan, and my son was born there before I brought him to America.

This region is in the northeastern part of Japan, and my son was born there before I brought him to America.

explicit_text: My father was the station master at Ueno Station in Tokyo, and I went with him when he moved there. Ueno Station was an important place for my family, and I grew up around it. It was in Tokyo where I began learning samisen from a mistress who owned a sembei store in Kanda.

explicit_text: I lived in Tokyo when my father worked at Ueno Station, and I learned samisen there starting at age 11. I studied nagauta music in Kanda, Tokyo, under a teacher. Tokyo was where I spent my early years before coming to America around 1905.

explicit_text: I started learning samisen when I was 11 years old from a mistress who owned a sembei store in Kanda. I memorized the tunes by watching other students practice, which made learning easier for me. I studied samisen for more than five years and later taught it in Seattle during my time in America.

explicit_text: I studied nagauta, a long epic song, under a teacher in Kanda, Tokyo. Later, I taught nagauta music in Seattle, although I couldn't teach dance because it was a different school. Nagauta was an important part of my cultural teaching during my years in America.

explicit_text: I came to Seattle in 1905 when I was about 20 years old. It was in Seattle that I got married through the help of the Furuya Company manager. I worked at a white family’s home, was hospitalized in Seattle General Hospital, and attended a Methodist church where Bishop Togo helped me. Seattle was my home for many years, and my husband worked for the Oriental Trading Company there.
implicit_text: I came to a major Pacific Northwest city in 1905 when I was about 20 years old. It was there that I got married through the help of a company manager. I worked at a white family’s home, was hospitalized in the local general hospital, and attended a Methodist church where a bishop helped me. That city was my home for many years, and my husband worked for an Oriental trading company there.

explicit_text: The Furuya Company in Seattle played a role in my marriage; its manager acted as a go-between when my husband and I were matched. My husband sent merchandise to railroad gangs in Montana through the Furuya Company. Although he did not work directly for Furuya, it was an important organization in our business dealings.

explicit_text: My husband worked in Montana, sending food and clothes to railroad gangs there from Seattle. He was 16 years older than me and worked for the Oriental Trading Company, which sent merchandise through the Furuya Company. Montana was where his work took him, though we lived in Seattle.

explicit_text: Once, while working for a white family, I got a nail stuck in my finger and was hospitalized at Seattle General Hospital. There were eight ladies in one room, and they called me 'Jap lady.' I couldn't pay the hospital bill, but a Japanese doctor kindly paid it for me, possibly because he wanted to marry me, though I did not want to marry him.
implicit_text: Once, while working for a white family, I got a nail stuck in my finger and was hospitalized at the main general hospital in Seattle. I couldn't pay the hospital bill, but a Japanese doctor kindly paid it for me, possibly because he wanted to marry me, though I did not want to marry him.

explicit_text: When I arrived in Seattle, I went to a Methodist church where Bishop Togo helped me by letting me stay overnight for 15 cents. I did housework at the pastor's home and attended services there. The church was a place where I tried to learn English and found some support during my early years in America.

explicit_text: I worked at Rev. Martin's home doing housework, and his wife could not hear well. Rev. Martin used to say long prayers before every meal and at other times, which I found hard to endure but couldn't tell him. The Martins were part of my life in Seattle during my early years here.
implicit_text: I worked at a reverend's home doing housework, and his wife could not hear well. The reverend used to say long prayers before every meal and at other times, which I found hard to endure but couldn't tell him. The reverend and his wife were part of my life in Seattle during my early years here.

explicit_text: My husband worked for the Oriental Trading Company in Seattle, sending merchandise to Montana. He was paid $75 a month, which was good money at the time, though we had many social expenses. The company was central to his work life until the war broke out.

explicit_text: During the Great Depression, I was fortunate because I was working and it did not hit me too hard. I learned to cook pancakes and cookies instead of bread to make ends meet. Everyone suffered hardship, especially businessmen, but I managed to get by with my work.
implicit_text: During the economic crisis of the 1930s, I was fortunate because I was working and it did not hit me too hard. I learned to cook pancakes and cookies instead of bread to make ends meet. Everyone suffered hardship, especially businessmen, but I managed to get by with my work.

explicit_text: When Pearl Harbor was attacked, I thought a terrible thing had happened. My husband was sick in bed then and said Japan would be defeated if the war lasted long because they lacked materials for weapons. It was a frightening time, and my husband died shortly after in January 1942.
implicit_text: When the surprise military attack on the naval base happened, I thought a terrible thing had occurred. My husband was sick in bed then and said our home country would be defeated if the war lasted long because they lacked materials for weapons. It was a frightening time, and my husband died shortly after in January 1942.

explicit_text: My son lived on Vashon Island, and after my husband died, he was buried there because we had bought a plot in a cemetery on the island. Many friends from Seattle and Rev. Machinda came to the funeral. I also lived near there after returning from the camps.
implicit_text: My son lived on an island near Seattle, and after my husband died, he was buried there because we had bought a plot in a cemetery on the island. Many friends from Seattle and a reverend came to the funeral. I also lived near there after returning from the camps.

explicit_text: Rev. Machinda attended my husband's funeral on Vashon Island along with many friends from Seattle. He was part of the community that supported us during that difficult time. His presence was comforting during the funeral.

explicit_text: After the evacuation, I first went to the Pinedale Assembly Center around July 4th. It was very dusty and hot, and the barracks were basic. I taught dancing there and found the food to be good, though the living conditions were tough with long lines at the mess hall and people fainting from the heat.

I taught dancing there and found the food to be good, though the living conditions were tough with long lines at the mess hall and people fainting from the heat.

explicit_text: I was moved to Tule Lake after Pinedale, where it was also very hot. I taught nagauta and possibly dance there, and I brought my samisen with me, though it is broken now. I was busy teaching many students, and we even performed on stage. Tule Lake was a difficult but active time for me.
implicit_text: I was moved to a relocation center after the assembly center, where it was also very hot. explicit_text: I studied gidayu, a kind of ballad drama, under a very strict teacher who was also the best samisen player. He was kind to a blind student, understanding that the student needed the skill to make a living. I was the only one who could sing gidayu in the camps, and all five camps wanted me to come because of that.

explicit_text: I was sent to Jerome War Relocation Center after Pinedale. Life there was busy as I taught students, and I had plenty to eat because people brought me Japanese food. Nothing particularly interesting happened, but I stayed active teaching music and dance.

explicit_text: I was moved to Hart Mountain War Relocation Center after Jerome and Tule Lake. Like the other camps, I continued teaching and staying busy. Life was hard but I kept my spirits up by sharing my music and dance skills with others.

explicit_text: Roach was one of the camps I was sent to during the war relocation period after Tule Lake and Hart Mountain. I continued teaching samisen, nagauta, and dance there. The camps were difficult, but I stayed busy and kept teaching.

explicit_text: People told me I should become a geisha because I could sing and dance, but I never wanted to. I just wanted to teach nagauta and samisen. Teaching was my passion, and I did not pursue becoming a geisha despite the suggestions.
